1.1: From James — a slave bound to God and to the Lord Jesus Christ — to the twelve tribes of Israel living in exile among the nations: Peace to you.
1.2: Welcome it as nothing but joy, my brothers and sisters — even when you find yourselves caught in trials of every kind.
1.3: For you know this: when faith is tested, it forges endurance — the kind that holds under sustained pressure.
1.4: Give endurance room to do its full work in you, and you will come through complete and mature — with no weak places left.
1.5: Does any of you feel you lack wisdom? Ask God. He gives generously to everyone who asks and never utters a word of reproach — and he will give it.
1.6: Only ask in faith, with no second-guessing — because the person who doubts is like a wave on the open sea, driven wherever the wind takes it.
1.7: A person like that should not imagine the Lord will give him anything at all.
1.8: He is split down the middle — pulled two ways at once — and that instability runs through everything he does.
1.9: The follower of Jesus who is poor in the world's eyes should boast in his true dignity before God —
1.10: and the one who is rich should take pride in being brought low — for wealth fades like a wildflower on the hillside.
1.11: The sun comes up blazing and burns off the grass; the blossom drops and its beauty is gone. So the rich man — for all his busy striving — will wither away in mid-stride.
1.12: How fortunate is the person who holds firm under trial! Once proven, he will receive the victor's crown — the life that belongs to the age to come — which God has promised to everyone who loves him.
1.13: When desire pulls at you, never say that God is the source of it. God is untouched by evil and draws no one toward it.
1.14: The source is always within: each of us is tempted when our own craving hooks us and drags us off course.
1.15: Once desire conceives, it gives birth to sin. Once sin is fully grown, its own offspring is death.
1.16: Do not let anyone mislead you here, my dear friends.
1.17: Every good thing given and every complete gift streams down from above — from the Father who made the lights of heaven, in whom there is no shadow, no shift, no variation of any kind.
1.18: By his own free choice he brought us into being through the word that is true — so that we would stand as the firstfruits, the advance offering, of all his new creation.
1.19: Mark this well, my dear friends: be quick to listen and slow to open your mouth — and let anger take a long time to arrive.
1.20: For human rage never brings about what God's righteousness calls for.
1.21: Strip off every kind of moral squalor and rank wickedness, and with open humility welcome the word that has been rooted in you — the word that has power to save your whole selves.
1.22: Act on what the word says — do not merely take it in. Listening without doing is self-deception.
1.23: If you hear the word but do not act on it, you are like someone who glances at his own face in a mirror —
1.24: looks, walks away, and within moments has forgotten what he saw.
1.25: But the one who leans into the perfect law — the law of freedom — and keeps at it; the one who does not hear and forget but hears and acts: that person will find blessing in their obedience.
1.26: If someone thinks themselves devout but cannot govern their own tongue, they have only fooled themselves — whatever they call religion, it amounts to nothing.
1.27: True devotion — the kind that God the Father calls pure and without spot — looks like this: coming to the aid of orphans and widows in their suffering, and keeping yourself uncorrupted by the world's influence.

2.1: Brothers and sisters, you cannot hold the faith of our glorious Lord Jesus Christ and at the same time play favorites.
2.2: Imagine two people walk into your assembly: one wearing a gold ring and fine clothes, the other dressed in rags.
2.3: If you fawn over the well-dressed one and offer the best seat, then turn to the poor person and say 'Stand over there' or 'Sit on the floor at my feet' —
2.4: have you not divided people into classes and set yourselves up as judges — driven by the worst kind of bias?
2.5: Pay close attention, dear friends: Has not God himself chosen those whom the world calls poor to be rich in faith — to inherit the kingdom he promised to those who love him?
2.6: And yet you humiliate the very people God has honored. It is the wealthy who exploit you and haul you before the judges —
2.7: — the same people who slander the name of Christ that was spoken over you at your baptism.
2.8: Now, if you genuinely keep the royal law — 'Love your neighbor as yourself' — you are doing exactly right.
2.9: But the moment you show partiality, you break the law. The law itself puts you on trial and pronounces you a transgressor.
2.10: God's law is not a list of separate items — it is a whole. Break any single point and you have broken the law.
2.11: The same lawgiver who said 'Do not commit adultery' also said 'Do not murder.' You may keep one and shatter the other — but the lawgiver's authority stands behind both commands, and you have broken his law.
2.12: Live and speak as people who will be judged by the law of freedom — the law that both liberates and holds you accountable.
2.13: When the day of judgment comes, mercy will not be shown to those who showed none. But mercy wins — it stands in triumph over judgment.
2.14: What use is it, my friends, for someone to claim faith while showing nothing by way of deeds? Can that kind of dead faith save anyone?
2.15: Say a fellow believer — a brother or sister — is without clothes and going hungry.
2.16: If one of you says to them 'God bless you, stay warm, eat well' but hands them nothing — what good is the blessing?
2.17: Faith without deeds is exactly the same: dead on its own.
2.18: Someone might argue: 'You have faith; I have deeds — two separate things.' Then show me this faith of yours with no deeds to back it up. I will show you mine by everything I do.
2.19: You say you believe in one God — fine. The demons believe that too. And they do not just believe; they tremble with terror.
2.20: Are you willing to be shown something, you empty-headed person? Faith cut off from deeds is barren — completely useless.
2.21: Consider Abraham, the father of our people: was he not shown to be in right standing with God precisely by his actions — when he laid his son Isaac on the altar?
2.22: You see it plainly: his faith and his deeds worked as one. The deeds completed the faith — brought it to full expression.
2.23: The Scripture was thus proven true: 'Abraham trusted God, and it was counted as righteousness.' And he was given the title that tells us everything: Friend of God.
2.24: The lesson is plain: a person is vindicated — shown to be in right standing — by their deeds, not merely by a declaration of belief.
2.25: Or take Rahab, known as a prostitute: was she not also vindicated by what she did — by sheltering the Israelite scouts and sending them safely away by another road?
2.26: A body without breath in it is a corpse. Faith without action is no different — it is dead.

3.1: Think carefully before you take on the teacher's role, brothers—those who stand before others to instruct will face a stricter accounting before God.
3.2: We all make many mistakes. But if someone could perfectly control every word they speak, they would have mastered themselves entirely—the person who never missteps with the tongue can govern their whole being.
3.3: Think of it this way: a small metal bit placed in a horse's mouth gives the rider command over the entire animal.
3.4: Or consider ocean-going ships—massive hulls battered by gales, yet a tiny rudder turns the whole vessel wherever the helmsman decides to go.
3.5: The tongue works the same way: a small limb, yet its claims are enormous. And consider how much a single spark can destroy—an entire forest reduced to ash.
3.6: The tongue is fire—a whole cosmos of wickedness packed into one small member. It corrupts the entire person; it ignites the whole sweep of human existence; and the flame that burns in it was lit in Gehenna itself.
3.7: Humanity has subdued every creature on earth: wild beasts, birds of the air, crawling things, and the creatures of the sea.
3.8: But the tongue? No one can tame it. It is a restless, untameable evil loaded with lethal venom.
3.9: We use the very same tongue to bless God our Father and to curse the people he made in his own image.
3.10: Praise and curses pour from the same mouth. Brothers, this simply must not be.
3.11: No spring sends up both sweet and bitter water from the same source.
3.12: A fig tree cannot bear olives, brothers. A vine does not grow figs. And a salt spring cannot give fresh water. The mouth is no different.
3.13: Is any of you truly wise? Prove it not by argument but by the quality of your daily life—deeds carried out with the quiet confidence that genuine wisdom produces.
3.14: But if your heart is eaten up with bitter envy and the drive to get ahead at others' expense, do not pretend to wisdom—you would only be lying against the truth.
3.15: That kind of 'wisdom' has nothing heavenly about it—it belongs to the earth, to the merely human, to the demonic.
3.16: Wherever envy and self-seeking ambition take root, chaos follows—and every form of wickedness flourishes alongside them.
3.17: Wisdom from above is another thing entirely. It is, first of all, pure—and from that purity flows a peaceableness, a gentleness, a willingness to listen and yield, a life overflowing with mercy and tangible good, an evenhandedness toward all people, and a complete absence of pretense.
3.18: Those who work to make peace are planting seeds—and the harvest that grows from that sowing is a life of righteousness.

4.1: Where do the wars and fights that tear your community apart come from? Look inside—they come from the cravings that wage their own war within each of you.
4.2: You crave what you don't have—and so you trample others. You hunger for more—and when you can't get it, you go to war. You remain empty-handed simply because you never ask God.
4.3: And when you do ask, your request is refused—because your motive is self-gratification, not God's purposes.
4.4: You have been unfaithful—like a spouse who runs after a rival. Don't you understand that loving what the world loves means standing against what God loves? Whoever chooses the world as their closest companion has chosen to stand against God.
4.5: Has Scripture been speaking all along to no effect? God himself placed his Spirit within us, and that Spirit guards his claim on us with an intensity we can only call jealousy.
4.6: And yet he gives grace upon grace—grace lavish enough to overcome our divided loyalties. As Scripture puts it: God sets himself against the proud, but pours his favor out on the humble.
4.7: So bring yourself under God's authority. Take your stand against the devil—and he will run.
4.8: Come close to God, and he will come close to you. Wash the guilt from your hands—you have been living like people who do not know him. Purify your inner life—you have been serving two masters at once.
4.9: Feel the weight of what you have done. Let grief come in. Stop laughing as though nothing is wrong—there is real reason to mourn. Let your lightheartedness become solemnity.
4.10: Lower yourselves before the Lord—and he will raise you up.
4.11: Stop tearing each other down with your words, brothers. When you attack another believer or appoint yourself their judge, you are overstepping the law—you place yourself above it rather than under it, trading the doer's place for the judge's seat.
4.12: There is one Lawgiver, one Judge—the God who alone has both the right and the power to save or condemn. Who do you think you are, appointing yourself to judge the person next to you?
4.13: Listen to this kind of talk: 'We're heading to such-and-such city for a year—we'll trade, we'll turn a profit.' Sounds reasonable enough.
4.14: But you have no idea what tomorrow holds. Your life is a morning mist—visible for a moment, then gone without a trace.
4.15: What you should be saying is: 'If the Lord wills it, we will live to carry out our plans.'
4.16: Instead you strut about and brag as though your future were yours to command. That kind of self-congratulation is simply wrong.
4.17: Knowing the good and refusing to do it—that is its own kind of sin.

5.1: Now hear this, you wealthy: weep and howl—the suffering that is coming for you will justify every tear.
5.2: Your wealth has already begun to decay. Your fine clothing has been eaten by moths.
5.3: Your gold and silver have rusted away—and that rust is its own verdict against you, a fire that will consume your very bodies. This is what you have done in the final days of history: accumulated wealth while others went without.
5.4: Listen: the wages you withheld from the farm workers who harvested your crops—those unpaid wages are crying out. The workers' own cries have already reached the ears of the Lord of the heavenly armies.
5.5: You have spent your days on earth in ease and indulgence, fattening yourselves—all while the day of reckoning drew near, as cattle fatten themselves unaware before the slaughter.
5.6: You have brought charges against the innocent and put them to death—and they never fought back.
5.7: So wait patiently, brothers—wait for the Lord's return. Think of the farmer: he plants his precious crop and then waits through the long season, trusting that the autumn rains and the spring rains will come in their time.
5.8: You must have that same patience. Settle your hearts and hold steady—the Lord's return is close.
5.9: Stop airing your grievances against each other, brothers—unless you want to face judgment yourselves. The Judge is already standing at the door.
5.10: Look to the prophets, brothers—they spoke God's word and they suffered for it. Let their endurance be your model.
5.11: We call people blessed who held on through suffering. You know the story of Job's endurance—and you know how it ended. That ending reveals who the Lord is: boundlessly compassionate, overflowing with mercy.
5.12: Above everything else, brothers: no oaths. Don't swear by heaven, don't swear by earth, don't swear by anything at all. A plain yes should mean yes, and a plain no should mean no—or you will find yourself standing under God's judgment.
5.13: Are you suffering? Pray. Are you joyful? Sing.
5.14: Is anyone among you ill? Call the church elders. Let them gather and pray over the sick person, anointing them with oil as they invoke the name of the Lord.
5.15: The prayer that trusts God will heal the sick person—the Lord will lift them up. And if sin has burdened them alongside sickness, that too will be forgiven.
5.16: So make it a practice: confess your failings to each other, pray for each other. This is how healing comes—not in isolation but in community. And the prayer of someone who is right with God carries extraordinary force.
5.17: Elijah was as human as any of us—no more, no less. He prayed with everything he had that rain would stop, and for three and a half years the earth went without.
5.18: He prayed again, and the rains came, and the land yielded its harvest.
5.19: Brothers, if one of your number wanders away from the truth and another person draws them back,
5.20: that person should know what they have accomplished: they have rescued a soul from death and drawn a curtain over a vast catalogue of sins.
